title: Nano: Building A Castle - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of the Nano text editor, its shortcuts, and file editing capabilities

questions.question: How do you open a file called 'castle.sh' in the Nano editor?
questions.explanation: To open a file in Nano, simply type 'nano' followed by the filename: 'nano castle.sh'. If the file doesn't exist, Nano will create it when you save. Nano is a simple, user-friendly terminal text editor.

questions.question: Which keyboard shortcut saves (writes out) a file in Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+O (Write Out) saves the current file in Nano. After pressing Ctrl+O, Nano prompts for the filename and you press Enter to confirm. In Nano's notation, this is shown as ^O at the bottom of the screen.

questions.question: Which keyboard shortcut exits Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+X exits Nano. If you have unsaved changes, Nano will ask if you want to save before exiting. Ctrl+Z would suspend Nano (not exit), sending it to the background.

questions.question: How do you search for text in Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+W (Where Is) opens the search prompt in Nano. Type your search term and press Enter to find the first occurrence. Press Ctrl+W again and Enter to find the next occurrence. This is different from Vim's / command.

questions.question: How do you cut (remove) an entire line in Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+K cuts the current line in Nano (the line where the cursor is). The cut line is stored in the cut buffer and can be pasted with Ctrl+U. You can cut multiple lines by pressing Ctrl+K repeatedly.

questions.question: How do you paste (uncut) text in Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+U (Uncut) pastes the contents of the cut buffer at the cursor position. This works in conjunction with Ctrl+K (Cut). Note that Ctrl+V in Nano is for entering the next character literally, not paste.

questions.question: What does the `^` symbol represent in Nano's shortcut notation at the bottom of the screen?
questions.explanation: In Nano's shortcut notation, ^ represents the Ctrl key. So ^X means Ctrl+X, ^O means Ctrl+O, etc. The M- prefix represents the Alt (Meta) key in Nano shortcuts.

questions.question: How do you open a file at a specific line number (e.g., line 15) in Nano?
questions.explanation: The syntax 'nano +15 file.txt' opens file.txt and places the cursor at line 15. The +N flag before the filename tells Nano to start at that line number. This is useful for jumping directly to a known location.

questions.question: How do you go to a specific line number while already inside Nano?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+_ (Ctrl+Shift+Minus, also shown as Ctrl+/) opens the 'Go To Line' prompt in Nano. You can type a line number and press Enter to jump to that line. Ctrl+G displays the help text.

questions.question: What happens if you open a non-existent file with Nano?

options.text: Nano displays an error and refuses to open
Nano opens an empty buffer and creates the file when you save
Nano creates an empty file immediately on the disk
Nano opens the last file you edited instead

questions.explanation: When you open a non-existent file (e.g., 'nano newfile.txt'), Nano opens an empty editing buffer with the title showing '[ New File ]'. The file is only created on disk when you save with Ctrl+O.
